title: The Secret Of Getting Rich Is To Do These 3 Things
full_text: Today we look at the question of success. Okay, the question is who succeeds and why? Okay, so we we've done a lot of research and we've discovered that people who who succeed um succeed for certain reasons. Those who are stupid are often the most confident in the world. That's what what's called the Dunning Krueger effect. And this is this helps explain why the world is why it is because often the people in power are stupid. They don't know they're stupid. They were confident and they do stupid things like Donald Trump. Okay. Okay. You guys, you guys need to remember this. Just because things are correlated does not mean they cause each other. So, I'll give you an example. We know that successful people, they get up early in the morning. Okay? They get up at 4:00 in the morning. But just because you get up at 4:00 in the morning does not mean you'll succeed. Okay? Just because you work hard, just because you're resilient, just because you have growth mindset does not mean you'll succeed. You will have uh more resilience. You you'll be more humble. Okay, does that make sense? So if you're rich, guess what happens? You become successful and therefore you will have growth mindset, self-control, deliberate practice. Okay, does that make sense? But just because you have growth mindset delivered practice and resilience does not mean you'll succeed. Okay. So the question for us is why does this happen and ultimately who succeeds? Okay. So we know for a fact that rich people are much more likely to succeed than poor people. And in fact what we know from macroeconomic studies is that school doesn't really matter. Doesn't matter how well you do in school. If your parents are rich you'll be successful in life. If your parents are poor, you will not be successful in life. Okay, so let's think about this more carefully. Why doesn't school matter? Well, it's because rich people and poor people are playing different games with education. Okay, for rich people, college is about signaling. It's about the network, right? You go to Yale, you go to Harvard, you meet other rich kids, you make connections, and those connections get you jobs. The degree itself, it's just a signal that says, "I'm one of you." Okay? But for poor people, college is supposed to be about credentials. You're told, "Get perfect grades, get into a good school, and you'll succeed." But here's the problem. Even if you get perfect grades, you don't have the network. Okay? You don't know anyone who can give you a job. You don't have family friends who run companies. So you end up with a degree but no connections. And the connections are what actually matter. Does that make sense? So just like the marshmallow test isn't really about self-control, it's about trust. College isn't really about intelligence. It's about privilege. Okay? The marshmallow test measures whether you trust adults to keep promises. College measures whether you have the social capital to convert a degree into a career. Poor kids can be just as smart, work just as hard, but they don't have the network, so they don't get the same outcomes. And people look at this and say, well, education is the great equalizer. But it's not. It's actually a mechanism that reinforces inequality because it pretends to be fair while actually favoring those who already have advantages. Okay, so what's the difference? Okay, so now let's look at parenting strategies between rich and poor. Okay, so there are lots of differences, but let's focus on three major differences. The first major difference is that rich parents speak to their kids more than poor parents. Okay, high voca vocabulary, low vocabulary. When rich parents speak to the kids, rich parents will use higher vocabulary, longer sentences. Poor parents will just be no, yes, go away. Okay. Another major difference is in attitude. Okay. So, rich parents use a friendly attitude and poor parents use an authoritarian or command attitude. Okay, so the rich parent will spend a lot of time explaining to the child why this is wrong and how not to do it again. The poor parent is like, "Don't you ever do that again or I'll beat the crap out of you." Okay, don't do that. Pretty simple. Okay. But as you can you can understand the rich kid will understand that the world is safe and that he is respected in this world. Okay. Whereas the poor child will think that the world is scary and he or she must be afraid of adults. And this carries on into school, right? Because as a rich kid, you go into school and you think that oh my teacher is my friend. So you smile at the teacher and you hug the teacher and the teacher smiles back and now you're friends, right? But if you're a poor kid and you see the teacher, you don't you're afraid to look at the teacher. you don't smile, you're you are stressed. Okay? And the teacher thinks that you might be a problem child. So the problem with this is that yes, being a third parent, it is effective, but it creates stress in the family. Okay? It makes the child feel unsafe. And this leads to our the third major difference between rich parents and poor parents. Rich parents offer stability. Okay? Poor parents can only offer volatility. This is a very simple idea. Rich parents have money so they can afford to keep promises. So I'm rich parent. I say to my child, "Okay, next week we'll go to Thailand for vacation. Guess what? Next week you go, you guys go to Thailand for vacation." But if you are a poor parent, money is always an issue. So it's hard for you to keep promises, right? So, it's like next week we'll go to McDonald's for lunch, but your paycheck um is not enough. So, you're like, "Sorry, we can't go anymore." Okay. Self-control. What is the marshmallow test? Marshmallow test is not a test of self-control. It's a test of your trust in others. Right? But if you think that this teacher is lying to me, then you're not going to eat that marshmallow. Then you will eat that marshmallow. Right? So it's not really about self-control. It's about your belief and trust in authority figures, right? So, if you're rich, stability, you have stability. But if you're poor, you don't have stability. So, you're actually better off eating that marshmallow rather than waiting for that second marshmallow. Because guess what? Most of the time, you will not get that second marshmallow. Okay? Well, the idea of resilience is that you believe that the world will help you, right? If you're poor, if you fail, that probably tells you you should be doing this. Okay? You probably won't succeed doing this. So, you don't have you don't have much confidence in others. Okay? And the idea of self- assessment, right? Looking inward. And it's the case because society is a hierarchy. Okay? And the hierarchy is usually divided between the rich and the poor. Okay? And these two worlds are night and day. They're very, very different. When we talk about getting into trouble with the law, we're actually talking about two completely different justice systems. Okay? There's one system for the rich and one system for the poor. And they operate by totally different rules. So, if you're rich and you commit a crime, what kind of crime do you commit? Financial fraud, tax evasion, environmental violations, right? like dumping chemicals into a river or manipulating stock prices or hiding money offshore. Okay. And what happens when rich people do this? Well, usually nothing. And if something does happen, they negotiate a settlement. Does that make sense? This is called brain drain. The poor countries lose their best people and this makes those countries stay poor. So immigration which is supposed to be about opportunity actually perpetuates global inequality. The rich countries get richer by taking talent from poor countries. And the poor countries stay poor because their talent leaves. Okay? And we look at immigrants who succeed and we say, "See, the American dream works." But we're only seeing the survivors. We're only seeing the tiny percentage who made it. We don't see the thousands who tried and failed. We don't see the people who couldn't get visas. We don't see the people who died trying to cross the border. Okay? It's survivorship bias. We only count the successes and we ignore all the failures. And those who succeed are able to think strategically and far ahead and position themselves in a way that allows them to get lucky. Okay? And they don't really connect with each other. They have different parenting strategies. They have different outlooks. The rich stay with themselves. The poor stay amongst themselves. So, this looks like a very stable system, right? Stability or the word we can use is equilibrium. But here's the really important question. Why does the system stay stable? Why doesn't it change? And the answer is something called meritocracy. Okay, meritocracy is the belief that we live in a fair system where people succeed based on their merit, their talent, their hard work. And this belief is really, really important for keeping the system stable. Let me explain why. If you're rich, you need to believe that the system is fair. Because if the system isn't fair, then your wealth is illegitimate, right? Like if you admit that you only succeeded because your parents were rich, because you went to good schools, because you had connections. If you admit all that, then you have to admit that maybe you don't deserve what you have. Maybe you're not special, maybe you just got lucky. And that's really uncomfortable. Okay? So rich people convince themselves that they earned everything. I worked hard. I'm talented. I'm smart. That's why I succeeded. They have to believe this. It's a psychological necessity. And this is why you hear rich people say things like, "I'm self-made." Even when they clearly had advantages. They need to believe in meritocracy because otherwise they have to face the fact that the system is rigged in their favor. Does that make sense? But here's the really insidious part. Poor people also believe in meritocracy. Okay? And when poor people believe that the system is fair, what does that mean? It means that if you're poor, it's your fault. You didn't work hard enough. You're not smart enough. You're not talented enough. So poor people internalize their failure as a personal flaw rather than a systemic problem. They blame themselves instead of blaming the system. And this keeps them from organizing, from demanding change, from challenging the hierarchy. Okay, there's an alternative way of thinking about this called luck egalitarianism. Okay, luck egalitarianism says we should acknowledge the role of circumstances. We should admit that where you're born, who your parents are, what opportunities you have, that all of this is luck. And if it's luck, then maybe we should try to equalize things a bit. Maybe we should redistribute resources to compensate for bad luck. But we don't do this because we're too invested in the meritocracy myth. We want to believe that success is earned and failure is deserved. And this is why the system is in equilibrium. The rich believe they deserve their wealth, so they don't feel guilty and they resist redistribution. The poor believe they deserve their poverty, so they don't rebel and they keep trying to quote unquote work harder. Everyone is locked into their position. The hierarchy reproduces itself generation after generation. And it all depends on this shared belief in meritocracy, a belief that, as we've seen throughout this lecture, is fundamentally false. Okay. So what we have is a system that looks fair on the surface. We say everyone has equal opportunity. Anyone can succeed if they work hard. But underneath it's a rigged game. The rich and the poor are playing by different rules in different worlds. And the outcome is predetermined before the game even starts. And the belief in meritocracy, that's what keeps everyone playing the game instead of flipping over the table. Does that make sense, guys? Okay.
